tagline: Calculate equivalent exposure settings using the exposure triangle — aperture, shutter speed, and ISO
keywords: exposure calculator, exposure triangle, EV calculator, aperture shutter ISO

faq.question: What is exposure value (EV)?
faq.answer: EV is a single number representing a combination of aperture, shutter speed, and ISO. EV 0 equals f/1.0 at 1 second at ISO 100. Each +1 EV halves the light reaching the sensor.

faq.question: How do I use the exposure triangle?
faq.answer: The three settings — aperture, shutter speed, and ISO — are interconnected. Changing one requires adjusting another to maintain the same exposure. Opening the aperture by 1 stop lets you double the shutter speed.

faq.question: What EV is needed for outdoor photography?
faq.answer: Bright sunny days are EV 14-16. Overcast skies are EV 10-12. Sunset/sunrise is EV 8-10. Night photography typically requires EV 0 or below.

formulas: Equivalent Exposure: EV stays constant when stops balance out

tagline: Calculate golden ratio dimensions for photography composition and design
keywords: golden ratio, phi, 1.618, golden section, composition calculator

faq.question: What is the golden ratio?
faq.answer: The golden ratio (φ ≈ 1.618) is a mathematical proportion found in nature and art. Two quantities are in the golden ratio when their ratio equals the ratio of their sum to the larger quantity.

faq.question: How do I use the golden ratio in photography?
faq.answer: Place key subjects at golden ratio points (about 38.2% and 61.8% of the frame). This creates more dynamic compositions than centering, similar to but more refined than the rule of thirds.

faq.question: Is the golden ratio better than the rule of thirds?
faq.answer: They're similar — the rule of thirds divides at 33%/67%, while the golden ratio divides at 38.2%/61.8%. The golden ratio places subjects slightly closer to center, which many find more pleasing.

formulas: Golden spiral points: 38.2% and 61.8% from edges

tagline: Calculate flash-to-subject distance, required aperture, or flash power from the guide number
keywords: guide number, flash calculator, GN calculator, flash distance, flash power

faq.question: What is a flash guide number?
faq.answer: The guide number (GN) indicates flash power. It equals flash-to-subject distance × f-number at ISO 100. A GN of 36 means the flash can illuminate a subject 36 meters away at f/1 (ISO 100).

faq.question: How does ISO affect flash range?
faq.answer: Doubling ISO increases effective flash range by √2 (about 1.4×). Going from ISO 100 to 400 doubles your flash distance.

faq.question: What is a good guide number for a flash?
faq.answer: Built-in flashes: GN 8-12. Mid-range speedlights: GN 30-40. Professional speedlights: GN 50-60. Studio strobes can exceed GN 100.

tagline: Calculate the equivalent focal length and field of view for different sensor sizes
keywords: crop factor, equivalent focal length, sensor size, APS-C, field of view

faq.question: What is crop factor?
faq.answer: Crop factor is the ratio of a full-frame sensor diagonal (43.3mm) to your camera's sensor diagonal. APS-C sensors have a 1.5-1.6× crop factor, making a 50mm lens act like 75mm on full frame.

faq.question: Does crop factor affect aperture?
faq.answer: It doesn't change the actual aperture or exposure, but it does affect depth of field. A 50mm f/1.8 on APS-C gives similar DOF to a 75mm f/2.7 on full frame.

faq.question: What is the reciprocal rule?
faq.answer: For sharp handheld shots, use a shutter speed of at least 1/(equivalent focal length). A 200mm equivalent needs at least 1/200s. Image stabilization lets you go 2-5 stops slower.

formulas: Equivalent Focal Length = Actual FL × Crop Factor
Equivalent Aperture (DOF) = Actual Aperture × Crop Factor
Min Shutter Speed = 1 ÷ Equivalent Focal Length

tagline: Estimate file sizes for RAW, JPEG, TIFF, and PNG images at any resolution
keywords: photo file size, image size calculator, RAW file size, JPEG size estimate

faq.question: How big is a RAW photo file?
faq.answer: RAW file sizes depend on megapixels and bit depth. A 24MP camera at 14-bit produces ~25-30MB RAW files (compressed). A 45MP camera produces ~50-60MB RAW files.

faq.question: Why are RAW files so much larger than JPEG?
faq.answer: RAW files store all sensor data with minimal compression (12-14 bits per channel). JPEGs use lossy compression and only 8 bits per channel, reducing size by 5-10×.

faq.question: How many photos fit on a 64GB memory card?
faq.answer: At 24MP: ~2,100 RAW files, ~8,500 high-quality JPEGs. At 45MP: ~1,100 RAW, ~4,500 JPEGs. Shooting RAW+JPEG uses both amounts.

tagline: Calculate the right shutter speed for sharp photos based on focal length, motion, and conditions
keywords: shutter speed, motion blur, sharp photos, reciprocal rule, night photography

faq.question: What shutter speed stops motion blur?
faq.answer: For still subjects: 1/focal length (equivalent). Walking people: 1/250s. Sports: 1/500-1/1000s. Birds in flight: 1/2000s+. Cars: 1/1000-1/4000s.

faq.question: What is the reciprocal rule?
faq.answer: Use a shutter speed of at least 1/(equivalent focal length) to avoid camera shake blur. A 200mm lens on APS-C (300mm equiv) needs at least 1/300s without stabilization.

faq.question: How many stops does image stabilization help?
faq.answer: Modern lens IS provides 2-4 stops of stabilization. In-body stabilization (IBIS) provides 3-7 stops. This means a 1/250s minimum could become 1/30s with 3-stop IS.

formulas: Minimum Speed = Equivalent Focal Length × Motion Factor
Motion Factors: Still=1×, Walking=2×, Sports=4×, Fast action=8×

tagline: Calculate the right amount of wood glue for your joint area and project
keywords: wood glue amount, glue coverage, woodworking adhesive, glue calculator

faq.question: How much wood glue do I need?
faq.answer: Apply a thin, even coat covering the entire joint surface. PVA glue covers about 25 square inches per ounce. A standard 8oz bottle handles most small to medium projects.

faq.question: Should I apply glue to both surfaces?
faq.answer: For PVA (yellow) glue, applying to both surfaces gives the strongest bond. For polyurethane glue, apply to one surface and dampen the other with water.

faq.question: How long should I clamp wood glue?
faq.answer: PVA: 30-60 minutes minimum, 24 hours for full cure. Polyurethane: 1-2 hours clamp, 24 hours full cure. Epoxy: varies by formula, 4-24 hours.

formulas: Total Area = Joint Length × Joint Width × Number of Joints × 2 (both surfaces)
Glue Needed (oz) = Total Area ÷ Coverage Rate
PVA coverage ≈ 25 sq in/oz, Polyurethane ≈ 20 sq in/oz

tagline: Calculate miter and bevel angles for any polygon, corner, or compound joint
keywords: miter angle, miter saw angle, corner angle, compound miter, bevel angle

faq.question: What miter angle do I need for a 90-degree corner?
faq.answer: For a standard 90-degree corner (like a picture frame), set your miter saw to 45 degrees. Each piece gets cut at 45°, and together they form the 90° corner.

faq.question: How do I calculate miter angles for a polygon?
faq.answer: Miter angle = (180 - interior angle) ÷ 2. Interior angle = (n-2) × 180 ÷ n, where n is the number of sides. For a hexagon (6 sides): interior = 120°, miter = 30°.

faq.question: What is a compound miter?
faq.answer: A compound miter combines a miter angle (horizontal) with a bevel angle (vertical tilt). It's needed for crown molding, sloped frames, and tapered boxes. Both angles change when a tilt is introduced.

tagline: Calculate wood movement from moisture content changes for gap-free joinery
keywords: wood shrinkage, wood movement, moisture content, wood expansion, seasonal movement

faq.question: How much does wood shrink when it dries?
faq.answer: Wood shrinks about 1% for every 4% change in moisture content (varies by species). A 6-inch red oak board might shrink 0.06" going from 12% to 8% MC — enough to open joints.

faq.question: Does wood move more in some directions?
faq.answer: Yes. Tangential shrinkage (across growth rings) is about twice radial shrinkage. Quartersawn boards move roughly half as much as flatsawn boards across their width.

faq.question: What moisture content should furniture wood be?
faq.answer: For indoor furniture in most US climates: 6-9% MC. Heated homes in winter can drop to 4-6%. Outdoor projects should be at 12-16% MC depending on climate.

formulas: Shrinkage % = Species Tangential % × (MC Change ÷ Fiber Saturation Point)
Fiber Saturation Point ≈ 19% for most hardwoods
Seasonal Range ≈ Width × Tangential% × (4% MC swing ÷ 19)

tagline: Calculate even dovetail pin and tail spacing for beautiful hand-cut joints
keywords: dovetail spacing, dovetail layout, pin spacing, tail spacing, dovetail joint

faq.question: What angle should I use for dovetails?
faq.answer: Use 1:8 (about 7°) for general purpose, 1:7 for hardwoods, and 1:6 (about 9.5°) for softwoods. Steeper angles in softwood prevent the pins from splitting.

faq.question: How many tails should I use?
faq.answer: A general guide is one tail per inch of board width, minus one. A 6-inch board typically gets 3-5 tails. More tails look refined; fewer look bold and rustic.

faq.question: How wide should half-pins be?
faq.answer: Half-pins (at the edges) should be at least 1/4" wide but no more than 2/3 the full pin width. Common practice is 3/8" for furniture-scale work.

formulas: Usable Width = Board Width - 2 × Half-Pin Width
Tail Spacing = Usable Width ÷ Number of Tails

tagline: Calculate how much wood stain or finish you need for your project
keywords: wood stain coverage, stain calculator, finish coverage, polyurethane calculator

faq.question: How much area does a quart of stain cover?
faq.answer: Oil-based stain: 200-300 sq ft/qt. Water-based stain: 250-350 sq ft/qt. Polyurethane: 300-400 sq ft/qt. Rough or porous wood absorbs more and reduces coverage.

faq.question: How many coats of stain do I need?
faq.answer: Most stains need 1-2 coats. Polyurethane needs 2-3 coats with light sanding between. Deck stain typically needs 2 coats. Apply thin, even coats rather than one thick coat.

faq.question: Should I buy quarts or gallons?
faq.answer: Gallons are typically 20-30% cheaper per ounce than quarts. If you need more than 3 quarts, a gallon is usually more economical. Leftover stain stores well for touch-ups.

formulas: Total Coverage = Surface Area × Number of Coats × (1 + Waste %)
Quarts Needed = Total Coverage ÷ Coverage per Quart

tagline: Calculate lumber costs using board feet pricing for hardwood and dimensional lumber
keywords: lumber cost, board foot calculator, hardwood pricing, wood cost estimator

faq.question: What is a board foot?
faq.answer: A board foot (BF) is 144 cubic inches of wood — equivalent to a board 1 inch thick × 12 inches wide × 12 inches long. Hardwood lumber is priced per board foot.

faq.question: How much does hardwood lumber cost?
faq.answer: Common hardwoods: Poplar $3-5/BF, Red Oak $5-8/BF, Cherry $7-10/BF, Walnut $10-15/BF, Maple $5-8/BF. Exotic woods can exceed $20-50/BF.

faq.question: How do I calculate board feet?
faq.answer: Board Feet = (Thickness × Width × Length) ÷ 144, where all measurements are in inches. A 1" × 6" × 8' (96") board = 4 board feet.

formulas: Board Feet = (Thickness × Width × Length in inches) ÷ 144
Total Board Feet = BF per Board × Number of Boards
Total Cost = Total Board Feet × Price per Board Foot

tagline: Find the right screw size, pilot hole, and clearance hole for any wood joint
keywords: wood screw size, pilot hole, clearance hole, screw gauge, screw length

faq.question: How long should a wood screw be?
faq.answer: The screw should be long enough to pass through the top piece and penetrate the bottom piece by at least 1-1.5× the top piece thickness. Two-thirds of the screw should be in the bottom piece.

faq.question: Do I always need a pilot hole?
faq.answer: In hardwood: always. In softwood: for screws #8 and larger, or near edges. Pilot holes prevent splitting, make driving easier, and create stronger joints.

faq.question: What is a clearance hole?
faq.answer: A clearance hole in the top piece allows the screw shank to pass freely, so the screw threads only grip the bottom piece. This pulls the two pieces tightly together.

formulas: Recommended Length = Top Thickness + 1.5 × Top Thickness
